banner: Week 27 · Prove It — Congruent Triangles & CPCTC
essential_question: Why does the ORDER of letters in a congruence statement decide the correspondence instead of the picture — and how does CPCTC let you conclude a pair of parts is congruent without ever measuring them?
objective: Justify why vertex order — not visual position — determines a triangle correspondence, use CPCTC to conclude a specific pair of corresponding parts is congruent, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on congruent triangles, correspondence, and CPCTC.

content.prompt: Without looking at your notes, sketch two triangles and write your own congruence statement for them (invent your own letters). Then list all six corresponding parts your statement implies — three sides, three angles. Circle the one part you're least sure about.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 23A & 23B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 23A/23B. We check the tricky ones: finding corresponding parts from a correspondence statement even when the two triangles cross or share a vertex, relabeling the SAME triangle starting from a different letter, and the CPCTC fill-in-the-blank. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: two triangles can look identical in a drawing, yet a congruence statement with the letters in the wrong order can still be FALSE. Argue it, using your own example of swapping two letters.

Claim 2: CPCTC only runs in ONE direction — from 'the triangles are congruent' TO 'every corresponding part is congruent' — never the reverse for just one matching pair. Argue why finding ONE congruent pair of parts can't, by itself, prove the whole triangles are congruent.
Claim 3: given △XYZ ≅ △PQR, argue why ∠Z ≅ ∠R must be true even though nobody measured either angle directly.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 24 — Two-Column Proofs: SSS & SAS — where you'll prove triangles congruent using just three matching parts instead of checking all six, then use CPCTC to unlock the rest.

success_criteria.text: I can state the definition of congruent triangles and identify a valid congruence statement without help
I can name every corresponding side and angle from a congruence statement, even for a non-obvious or shared-vertex correspondence
I can use CPCTC to conclude that a specific pair of corresponding parts is congruent

options: they look about the same size
all three corresponding sides are congruent, but the angles don't matter
all three corresponding sides AND all three corresponding angles are congruent
questions.answer: all three corresponding sides AND all three corresponding angles are congruent
questions.explain: Congruence is a full six-part guarantee — three side pairs and three angle pairs — not a vague 'looks similar' impression.
RS is the 1st-2nd letters of RST; the matching side is the 1st-2nd letters of UVW — UV.

questions.prompt: Given △JKL ≅ △NML (the two triangles share vertex L). Name: (a) the angle that corresponds to ∠K, (b) the side that corresponds to JL.
questions.explain: Reading in order: J↔N, K↔M, L↔L. (a) ∠K is the 2nd letter, so it corresponds to ∠M. (b) JL is the 1st-3rd letters, so it corresponds to NL.

questions.prompt: If △PQR ≅ △STU is true, which of these is ALSO automatically true about the same two triangles?
questions.explain: Swapping the 1st and 2nd letters (P and Q) on one side requires swapping the 1st and 2nd letters (S and T) on the other, to preserve the exact same correspondence.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw two congruent triangles in different positions, label them with a valid correspondence of your choice, and mark all six corresponding parts (tick marks for sides, arc marks for angles) consistently.
questions.answer: Two same-size triangles, fully labeled with a self-consistent correspondence, all three corresponding sides tick-marked alike and all three corresponding angles arc-marked alike.
questions.explain: Rubric: two clearly congruent triangles drawn and labeled with a valid correspondence (1 pt) + all six corresponding parts correctly and consistently marked (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Complete the proof. Given: △RST ≅ △UVW. Prove: ∠S ≅ ∠V. Statements: (1) △RST ≅ △UVW. (2) ∠S ≅ ∠V. Fill in the REASON for each of the two statements above.
questions.answer: (1) Given. (2) CPCTC — S is the 2nd letter of RST and V is the 2nd letter of UVW, so ∠S and ∠V are corresponding angles, and corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent.
questions.explain: This is the exact payoff move you'll use in every proof starting next lesson: once congruence is established (Given, or proven), CPCTC hands you any one specific corresponding part you still need.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 23 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit before Lesson 24's SSS & SAS proofs.
